CITY MANAGER'S OFFICE 
2180 MIL VIA STREET 
BERKELEY, CALIFORNIA 94704 
August 21, 1979 
City of Berkeley 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Ted Prim, Chief 
Conflicts of Interest Division 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 
95804 
Re: 
Request for an Advisory Opinion 
Dear Mr. Prim: 
Request No,_. -----=~==-
o 
LJ r:;C 
Date Received 0 -- Q 
. ! _i 
o ,-; 
~n~ 
Duei __ ~7_·-~r ____ _ 
PROPOSED RESPONSE 
DUE: 
9-7' 
I have been directed by the City Council of the City of Berkeley to 
request from the Fair Political Practices Commission an opinion as to 
the appropriateness of the City of Berkeley requiring that community-
based organizations performing social services, as contracted for by 
the City, consider and adopt Conflict of Interest Codes and/or request 
exemption from disclosure requirements. 
The City Council also wishes 
to be advised as to the authority of the Council to exempt, categorically, 
from the Conflict of Interest Code process contract agencies which 
receive from the City less than specified levels of funding, or which have 
less than a stated minimum annual operating budget. 
On October 25, 1977, the Berkeley City Council initially adopted a process 
for the adoption of Conflict of Interest Codes by intra-City agencies. 
The Council held a public hearing on March 21, 1978 for the purpose of 
considering Conflict of Interest Codes adopted by intra-City agencies. 
During the review of these Conflict of Interest Codes questions were 
raised by members of the City Council. 
On June 23, 1978, Berkeley City Clerk Edythe Campbell and currently Acting 
City Attorney Ted Lakey met with Dwight Dickerson of your staff. 
Much was 
accomplished as a result of the June 23, 1978 meeting with Mr. Dickerson, 
and on January 9, 1979 the Berkeley City Council adopted a revised process 
and timetables for the consideration and adoption of Conflict of Interest 
Codes for City employees and members of City advisory boards and commissions. 
At the January 9 meeting the City Council also directed the City Manager to 
request the advisory opinion which is summarized at the beginning of this 
letter. 


The Political Reform Act of 1974 requires that all agencies of local 
governments consider and adopt Conflict of Interest codes requiring 
the disclosure of certain financial interests by persons who participate 
in decision-making. 
The City of Berkeley has established a recent 
history of contracting with local organizations for the provision of 
public services which are funded by City monies. 
The question raised 
by members of the City Council is, "Does the 1974 Act apply to these 
community organizations and persons who perform public services by means 
of contracts with the City of Berkeley?" 
The City currently supports two general types of community agency contract-
ing. 
The City participates in CETA programs which allow the City to 
contract for Public Service Employment projects as well as Education and 
Training projects. 
The City also is contracting with community agencies 
funded under the following non-CETA programs: 
1. 
Community Services Administration (CSA) (a federally-funded program 
for the poor - U. S. Community Services Administration). 
2. 
State Title VII Nutrition (a state-funded program to provide meals 
for senior citizens - State Department of Aging). 
3. 
County Short-Doyle (drug) (a county-funded program for outpatient 
drug intervention program - County of Alameda), 
4. 
County Short-Doyle (Mental Health) (a county-funded program for 
outpatient mental health services - County of Alameda). 
5. 
Community Development Block Grant (CDBG) (a federally-funded 
program - U. S. Department of Housing and Urban Development). 
6. 
General Fund (Tax generated money paid out directly by the City 
of Berkeley). 
The majority of the agencies receiving funds in the above categories are 
also receiving CETA funds. 
For your assistance and information I am listing below examples of, and 
descriptions of, agencies which receive CETA as well as non-CETA funding 
from the City. 
It is hoped that this listing will provide sufficient 
information for you to assess whether there is a need for these types of 
organizations to comply with the Political Reform Act of 1974, and, if so, 
to what extent and how. 
Please contact Ted Lakey, Acting City Attorney, 
at (415) 644-6380 if you need any additional information regarding any of 
these agencies of any of the issues raised by this letter. 
2. 


CETA CONTRACTS 
1. 
Public Service Employment 
A Public Service Employment contract can be operated only by a non-
profit, private or public agency. 
The City of Berkeley contracts 
with a non-profit agency to hire Public Service Employees (PSE) to 
work at the agency, assisting the agency in providing additional 
public services. 
The PSE contract with the agency limits 90% or 
more of the total budget to only salaries and fringe benefits paid 
to the PSE employee. 
The PSE employees can only be hired from among 
CETA-qualified Berkeley residents who are screened for eligibility 
and referred to the agency by a separate CETA-funded Basic Enrollee 
Services program. 
In sum, the agency can only hire from among the 
CETA-certified clients referred to the agency. 
10% or less of the 
budget is allocated to discretionary expenditures, such as for the 
administration time required to contract with the City and supervision 
and training the PSE trainee. 
The utilization of the less than 10% 
of the total budget which can be used for purposes other than salaries 
and benefits to the PSE employee is itemized in the City of Berkeley 
contract, is reported to the City during the contract period, 
and is 
subject to audit. 
PSE projects are operated by a wide variety of 
private-non-profit agencies. 
The agencies vary in total budget, length 
of incorporation, size of Board, and number of non-CETA staff. 
Currently Berkeley contracts with over fifty different agencies for 
PSE programs, ranging from the American Red Cross to the Berkeley 
Repertory Theatre. 
A few examples will follow. 
2. 
Education and Training Programs 
This second type of CETA program provides education and training 
services to CETA-certified clients. 
These projects include vocational 
classroom training, on-the-job training, English-language and basic 
education, and job development and placement of clients. 
In these 
programs, the City enters into a contract with the agency for the 
personnel and operating costs necessary to operate the education or 
training program. 
The agency hires staff, procures supplies, and 
operates an education or training program for Berkeley residents. 
The contract specifies the authorized expenditures; the agency reports 
these expenditures; and the City Auditor audits to determine compliance 
with the authorized budget and the CETA Rules and Regulations. 
The 
education and training programs vary from very small projects operated 
for less than $20,000/year to very large projects serving over 100 
clients with a budget of $200,000/year. 
The agencies operating the 
projects vary from public training institutions, to non-profit community 
organizations, to private-for-profit business people offering a training 
program. 
Currently, there are approximately twenty education and 
training program subcontractors. 
A few examples of these programs are 
also summarized below. 
3. 


3. 
Examples of Public Service Employment Contract 
a. 
Berkeley Community Health Project 
This agency is a non-profit, tax exempt corporation established 
in 1970 to provide medical, psychiatric and counseling services. 
Total annual budget is approximately $213,083 of which $22,628 
or 10.6% is provided by Berkeley CETA. 
Contracts with Berkeley 
CETA provide for the hiring of three part-time PSE Community Health 
Workers, fringe benefits, and administrative costs. 
Berkeley 
Community Health Project has a 5-member volunteer Board of Directors. 
b. 
Berkeley Community YWCA 
This agency is a non-profit, tax exempt corporation established in 
1940. 
The main thrust of the YWCA is the Women's Refuge, a year-
round emergency housing program providing free overnight shelter 
and meals to low-income women and their children during times of 
crlS1S. 
Total annual budget is approximately $183,875 of which 
$71,617 or 38.9% is provided by Berkeley CETA. 
Contracts with 
Berkeley CETA provide salaries, fringe benefits and administration 
of 8 PSE positions. 
Berkeley Community YWCA has a 30-member volunteer 
Board of Directors. 
c. 
Berkeley Repertory Theater 
This agency is a non-profit, tax-exempt corporation established in 
1968 to provide theatrical performances and training classes for the 
education and literary benefit of the general public and under-
privileged youth. 
Total annual budget is approximately $233,362 
of which $36,233 or 15.5% is provided by Berkeley CETA. 
Contracts 
provide for salaries, fringe benefits and administration of five 
PSE enrollees - two actor/directors, two theatre technicians, and 
a box office receptionist. 
Berkeley/Repertory Theatre has a 23-
member volunteer Board of Directors. 
d. 
Family Service of Berkeley 
This agency is a non-profit, tax-exempt corporation established in 
1907 to give the community of Berkeley and adjacent areas a program 
of professional welfare services. 
Total annual budget is approximately 
$144,296 of which $23,590 or 16.3% is provided by Berkeley CETA. 
Contract provides for salaries, fringe benefits and administration 
costs for two PSE Family Advocate Specialists. 
Family Service of 
Berkeley has a 19-member volunteer Board of Directors. 
e. 
West Berkeley Health Center 
This agency is a non-profit, tax-exempt corporation established in 
1970 to provide health care to indigent residents of South and West 
Berkeley. 
Total annual budget is approximately $127,848 of which 
$35,905 or 28% is provided by Berkeley CETA. 
Contracts with Berkeley 
CETA provide for the hiring of two PSE Community Health Workers and a 
PSE Bookkeeper, fringe benefits for these employees, and administrative 
costs. 
West Berkeley Health Center has a 16-member volunteer Board of 
Directors. 


4. 
Examples of Education Training Contract 
a. 
Bay Area Urban League 
This agency is a non-profit, tax-exempt corporation established in 
1953 to solve problems arising out of racial and ethnic inequities. 
Total annual budget is approximately $2,762,197 of which $776,153 
or 28% is provided by Berkeley CETA. 
Contracts cover activities 
of Basic Enrollee Services, Buy-In Skills Training, On-the-Job 
Training, and Job Search. 
PSE contracts cover the following PSE 
positions: 
Job Search Assistants, Job Search Advisors, Manpower 
Assistants, Follow-up Clerks, and Administrative Secretary. 
Bay 
Area Urban League has a 39-member volunteer Board of Directors. 
b. 
Asians for Job Opportunities in Berkeley (AJOB) 
This agency is a non-profit, tax-exempt corporation established in 
1975 to assist unemployed, underemployed and low-income Asians, 
especially those with limited proficiency in English, with 
vocational language training and/or pre-employment training. 
Total 
annual budget is approximately $224,993 of which $185,068 or 82.3% 
is provided by Berkeley CETA. 
Title I contracts cover ESL 
Preparation, Job Placement, On-the-Job Training, and Support Services. 
A PSE contract covers wages, fringe benefits and administration costs 
for an Intake and Referral Worker. 
AJOB has a l3-member volunteer 
Board of Directors. 
CONTRACTORS RECEIVING NON-CETA FUNDS 
1. 
Berkeley Community Health Project 
This agency receives $9,780 or 4.5% in CSA funds and $27,830 or 13% of 
County Short-Doyle (Drug) funds. 
City funding totals 28.1% of funds 
received (this includes CETA funds). 
The purpose of the drug program 
is to prevent the use/abuse of drugs by offering an information and 
referral system that provides both information about specific drugs 
and an overall support system to help meet the basic emotional and 
survival needs of people involved with drugs. 
The services of the CSA 
program is similar to the CETA program as described above. 
CSA funding 
covers certain non-personnel costs, such as rent/space, consumable 
supplies, insurance; and the drug program funds two drug coordinators, 
as well as certain non-personnel costs. 
2. 
West Berkeley Health Center 
This agency receives $57,750 or 45% in CSA funds and $25,000 or 19.5% 
in General funds. 
City funding totals 92.5% of funds received (this 
includes CETA funds). 
The services have been described above. 
CSA 
funds 50% of the Director, 75% of the Clinic Coordinator and various 
percentages of four other professionals plus fringe benefits, as well 
as certain non-personnel costs. 
The General Fund contract provides 
for an outreach coordinator and secretary plus fringe benefits and 
office/medical supplies. 
The General Fund monies are matched by county 
funds. 
5. 


3. 
Asians for Job Opportunities in Berkeley 
This agency receives $2,500 or 1% in CSA funds and $7,945 or 3.5% in 
County Short-Doyle Health (Drug) funds. 
City funding totals 86.8% of 
funds received (this includes CETA funds). 
The CSA funds cover fee-
for-service consultants and certain non-personnel costs; the drug contract 
funds a coordinator, fringe benefits, and rent/utilities/supplies. 
The 
drug contract deals with a drug prevention and intervention program to 
Asian youths. 
4. 
CHANGE, Inc. 
CHANGE, Inc., is a non-profit, tax-exempt corporation established in 
June, 1974, to provide mental health services to low-income and ethnic 
minorities of the South-West Berkeley community. 
Total annual budget 
is approximately $76,313 of which $28,970 or 38% is funded by CSA; 
$19,398 or 25% is funded by CETA PSE; $7,945 or 10.4% is funded by 
County Short-Doyle Health (drug) and $10,000 or 13% is funded by 
County Short-Doyle Health (Mental Health). 
City funding totals 86.4% 
of funds received. 
The drug contract provides evaluations, assessments, 
treatment plans for clients, as well as information, referral and 
counseling. 
CSA and mental health contracts provide evaluations, 
assessments, counseling, psychotherapy, outreach, medication, etc. 
CHANGE, Inc., has an 8-member board. 
5. 
East Bay Japanese for Action, Inc. (EBJA) 
East Bay Japanese for Action, Inc., is a non-profit, tax exempt corpora-
tion established in April, 1972, to provide a comprehensive program of 
social services to the non-English speaking Japanese elderly living in 
the East Bay. 
Total annual budget is approximately $79,624 of which 
$21,711 or 27.3% is funded by State Title VII Nutrition; $17,905 or 
22.5% is funded by CETA PSE, and $17,580 or 22% is funded by CSA. 
City 
funding totals 71.8% of funds received. 
All three contracts provide 
the same services as stated above. 
The Nutrition contract also provides 
Japanese meals five days per week for 250 days per year for approximately 
60 Japanese-American seniors. 
EBJA has a 9-member board. 
6. 
Center for Independent Living, Inc. (CIL) 
Center for Independent Living, Inc., is a non-profit, tax-exempt 
corporation established on March 3, 1972, to provide comprehensive 
services for the disabled and blind. 
The prime objective of CIL is 
to increase the disabled individual's capacity for independent living 
through support services, training and educational programs for clients, 
families and agencies. 
Total annual budget is approximately $11,647,240 
of which $51,000 or .4% is funded by Community Development Block Grant; 
$81,052 or .7% is funded by CETA PSE; $76,130 or .7% is funded by CSA. 
City funding totals 1.8% of funds received. 
The CDBG contract provides 
services to assist the disabled and elderly find suitable, accessible 
housing in Berkeley; the CSA contract provides services to the blind 
6. 


and partially sighted individuals. 
Both CSA and CDBG contracts provide 
salaries, fringe benefits, and certain non-personnel costs; 
the CETA 
PSE contract provides salaries, fringe benefits and administration of 
13 PSE positions. 
CIL has a 10-member board. 
7. 
Community Conservation Centers (CCC) 
Community Conservation Centers is a non-profit, tax-exempt corporation 
established to educate people about the connection between what they 
buy and consume and the waste it produces and to help them realize 
that their consumption is directly connected to the exploitation of 
the world's resources. 
CCC does not have a current contract with the 
City; however, in 1977-78 a General Fund contract was awarded in the 
amount of $33,600 plus $28,870 in in-kind contribution. 
As far as we 
can determine, the total annual budget was approximately $75,600. 
The 
General Fund contract exclusive of in-kind contribution is approximately 
44% of the annual budget. 
This agency is unique in that it had 38 part-
time workers who all voted on the operation of the Center. 
CCC also 
had a 6-member board of directors. 
Currently, the Department of Public 
Works is providing approximately $38,325 as a direct subsidy by providing 
the truck, salary of driver, and certain non-personnel costs. 
No 
contract covers this arrangement. 
8. 
YMCA New Light Senior Citizens Center 
The 
YMCA New Light Senior Citizens Center is a non-profit, tax-exempt 
corporation established in April 1975 to provide social services, such 
as jobs, nutrition, recreation, education, health services information, 
and referrals to seniors. 
The total annual budget is approximately 
$44,150 of which $26,220 or 59.4% is funded by CSA and $17,930 or 40.6% 
is funded by CETA PSE. 
City funding totals 100% of funds received. 
The 
CSA contract funds the director, part-time bookkeeper, rent, insurance 
and consumable supplies; the CETA contract provides salaries, fringe 
benefits and administration of three PSE positions. 
This agency has a 
9-member board. 
an opinion from your office by November 
Thank you for 
in this matter. 
Acting City Manager 
cc: 
Theodore R. Lakey, Acting City Attorney 
Edythe Campbell, City Clerk 
7.

sections.question: Does the Political Reform Act of 1974 apply to community organizations and persons who perform public services by means of contracts with the City of Berkeley? Does the City of Berkeley have the authority to require that community-based organizations performing social services consider and adopt Conflict of Interest Codes and/or request exemption from disclosure requirements? Does the City Council have authority to exempt, categorically, from the Conflict of Interest Code process contract agencies which receive from the City less than specified levels of funding, or which have less than a stated minimum annual operating budget?
sections.question_synthetic: The City of Berkeley seeks FPPC guidance on whether the Political Reform Act of 1974 applies to community organizations contracted by the City to provide social services, and whether the City can categorically exempt contractor agencies based on funding thresholds from Conflict of Interest Code requirements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from the City of Berkeley to FPPC, not an FPPC response. Document contains detailed background on CETA contracts and various contractor agency examples. No FPPC conclusion section present. Document appears truncated at end.

Does the City of Berkeley have the authority to require that community-based organizations performing social services consider and adopt Conflict of Interest Codes and/or request exemption from disclosure requirements? Does the City Council have authority to exempt, categorically, from the Conflict of Interest Code process contract agencies which receive from the City less than specified levels of funding, or which have less than a stated minimum annual operating budget?

embedding.summary: The City of Berkeley's City Manager requests an advisory opinion from the FPPC regarding the applicability of the Political Reform Act of 1974 to community-based organizations contracted by the City to provide social services, including whether the City can require adoption of Conflict of Interest Codes and categorically exempt agencies based on funding levels.
